[ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Development of ICD-11 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Mood Disorders.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the Mood Disorders or other diagnoses presented to you, no diagnosis, or a diagnosis that you feel best describes the person in the vignette but is not one of the choices available.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral:  KF es un ingeniero civil de 38 años de edad, que acude por iniciativa propia al centro local de salud mental comunitaria. Se queja de dificultad para dormir en las últimas 2 semanas, lo que le ha generado dificultad para concentrarse en el trabajo.
Presenting Symptoms: KF se presenta a la entrevista mostrándose cansado, pero con buena higiene y atuendo. Reporta que desde hace dos semanas, todos los días se despierta en la mitad de la noche, teniendo dificultades para volverse a dormir. Comenta lo siguiente: “como resultado siento que me arrastro durante todo el día y me resulta muy difícil concentrarme en lo que se supone que debo de hacer”. Debido a que en su trabajo son muy importantes los detalles, le preocupa que vaya a cometer un error que pudiera causar serios problemas en el puente que está diseñando. Dice que en el trabajo se siente tenso y que constantemente está pensando en las formas en que podría incurrir en una equivocación. También se siente generalmente agitado e inquieto sin ser capaz de decir por qué. Cuando llega a su casa le es muy difícil relajarse y tiene molestias estomacales durante toda la noche. Por lo general le gustaba ver la televisión o jugar videojuegos por las tardes, pero recientemente pareciera que no puede reunir la energía para hacerlo. Estas actividades parecen no ser tan divertidas como lo solían ser. Su esposa se está molestando, ya que KF está dejando que las tareas del hogar que le corresponden a él se le acumulen, debido a que está demasiado cansado para poder hacerlas. Sus ojos se humedecen cuando refiere que está empezando a sentir como si su vida se estuviera desmoronando tanto en el hogar como en el trabajo. La última semana no finalizó dos trabajos a tiempo, sin embargo, su supervisor le dio un período de tiempo para ponerse al día. Sus crecientes problemas lo
 llevaron a pensar que necesita un poco de ayuda, motivo por el que acude a esta valoración.
Additional Background Information:   KF está felizmente casado desde hace 15 años. Hasta ahora siempre había funcionado muy bien en el trabajo. No tiene antecedentes psiquiátricos. Nunca había tenido un periodo de tiempo en el que se sintiera de manera similar. Refiere que no consume alcohol u otras drogas. En la exploración física que se le realizó en la visita inicial a la clínica, no se encontraron hallazgos anormales que pudieran explicar su condición actual.   

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette at the present time?
- Single Episode Depressive Disorder
- Recurrent Depressive Disorder
- Dysthymic Disorder
- Mixed Depressive and Anxiety Disorder
- Bipolar Type I Disorder
- Bipolar Type II Disorder
- Cyclothymic Disorder
- Other Mood Disorder
- Generalized Anxiety Disorder
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- A different diagnosis not listed above
- No Diagnosis; symptoms and behaviours are within normal limits

[FORMAT]
Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Second Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

**WARNING:** Any other output format is incorrect.

He says, “As a result I feel like I’m dragging through the whole day and it’s very hard to concentrate on what I’m supposed to do.” Because his job requires a lot of detail, he worries that he will make an error that could cause serious problems on the bridge he is designing. He says he feels tense at work and is constantly thinking about ways he might make a mistake. He also feels generally agitated and restless, though he can’t say why. When he gets home he has a hard time relaxing and experiences stomach discomfort all night. He usually liked watching TV or playing video games in the afternoons, but lately he seems unable to muster the energy to do so. These activities no longer seem as enjoyable. His wife is upset because KF is letting household chores pile up, as he is too tired to do them. His eyes water when he says he is starting to feel like his life is falling apart both at home and at work. In the last week he did not finish two jobs on time, but his supervisor gave him an extension. His growing problems have led him to think he needs help, which is why he is seeking this assessment.  

Additional background: KF has been happily married for 15 years. He has always worked well. He has no psychiatric history and has never had a period of feeling like this. Referral: NS, maestra de 43 años que vive con su esposo, su hija de 19 años y su hijo de 14 años, fue referida por su médico de atención primaria debido a sus quejas de tristeza e incapacidad para trabajar.
Presenting Symptoms: A lo largo la entrevista, el estado de ánimo de NS fue notablemente aplanado y su voz monótona fue baja y difícil de escuchar. Dice que sus problemas empezaron hace 7 meses cuando empezó a despertarse con regularidad a las 4 a.m. después de 5 horas de sueño y sin poder dormirse de nuevo. A pesar de estar despierta, menciona que no tenía  la energía o disposición para preparar el desayuno o de alistarse para la escuela. Al principio sí reconoció que estaba entrando “en otro círculo negativo” y se esforzaba por mantenerse enfocada pero fue progresivamente más difícil de combatir. Sus mañanas las pasó  cada vez más en cama, presentando rumiaciones acerca del trabajo de la escuela o  de los problemas de su hija. Refiere que a lo largo de los últimos 7 meses, frecuentemente recupera energía en la tarde o por la noche y logra hacer algunas tareas domésticas. Aproximadamente hace 2 meses, NS empezó a ausentarse a sus clases matutinas, y eventualmente dejó de ir a trabajar por completo. Ella expresa no tener ganas de hacer nada porque “parece nada tiene sentido”. Solía invertir sus horas libres de compras, cocinando o socializando con amigos. Sin embargo, menciona que no se ha esforzado por ver a sus amigos en semanas porque lo que solía ser divertido, como conversaciones vivaces, ahora parecen aburridas y dolorosas. NS también solía obtener un sentido de satisfacción cuando cocinaba un platillo nuevo o cuando preparaba una comida para su familia. Reporta que actualmente nada en su vida le brinda felicidad. El clínico que la entrevistó notó que durante la evaluación, sus movimientos y el habla fueron inusualmente lentos y pausados. Cuando fue cuestionada sobre si sus movimientos actuales son los habituales, ella confirma que siente como si estuviera moviéndose a través de lodo denso. NS come muy poco comparado con el año pasado y ha perdido 6.8 kg (15 lbs) desde que iniciaron los primeros síntomas. NS reporta que siente que no se recuperará en esta ocasión o que no volverá a trabajar nuevamente. Dice sentirse culpable por convertirse en una carga para otros y con frecuencia  desea estar muerta, en lugar de tener que vivir para ser testigo de tiempos tan horribles. No obstante, expresa que no ha tenido la energía para poner en práctica estos pensamientos.
Additional Background Information:  NS ha tenido tres episodios similares en los últimos 15 años, dos de los cuales fueron detonados cuando el trastorno alimentario de su hija de 19 años se exacerbó. Los dos primeros episodios se resolvieron completamente con tratamiento en un plazo de dos meses, pero el más reciente, que fue hace 2 años, duró cerca de seis meses. NS nunca ha experimentado episodios de alegría inapropiada, incremento en la actividad, o aumento en la autoestima. Un examen médico descartó cualquier condición médica que pudiera estar provocando sus síntomas. No hay indicio del abuso de drogas o alcohol.

RV is a 39‑year‑old housewife who presented to the local outpatient mental health clinic with complaints of sadness and apathy for the past three months. Her husband and 12‑year‑old son accompanied her to the appointment.

She lost by a narrow margin and has been mentally replaying her campaign strategy, convinced she could have done better. She spends most of the day thinking about her defeat and feels exhausted by the end of the day. She wakes frequently during the night and tends to go to bed later than before. Her husband notes that seeing her so distressed by the loss has been difficult, as she is usually very cheerful and energetic. He also points out that she has stopped attending local events such as her book club or religious meetings where she used to be an organizer. As the interview progressed, RG reported that for the past month he has been convinced that his colleagues are conspiring to remove him from his position. He believes that they will discover the money he has invested and will falsely implicate him in having disproportionate assets relative to his income, and that as a result he will be arrested. He admits that he has no evidence for this belief, except for having heard some rumors about the man who previously held his position and the government’s reasons for firing him. AC is a 24‑year‑old woman who presented to her primary care physician complaining of headache, lower back pain, and difficulty sleeping. She works as a sales associate at a local shopping mall and reports that her job is very stressful because of her demanding boss.

**Presenting Symptoms:**  
AC says that for the past four weeks she has felt sad more than usual, although she is still able to enjoy weekends. She reports that her sad mood comes and goes, but rarely lasts all day. She also feels quite anxious at work, with symptoms that include nausea and restlessness. When the mall is less busy, she can control her problems. Her headaches and lower back pain worsen when she is anxious. She notes that her boss has very high standards and frequently criticizes her work. On bad days she spends several hours worrying about being fired. These thoughts are distressing because she never finished high school and feels demoralized about her future job prospects. When the mall is busy, she sometimes feels overwhelmed and tension headaches force her to take the day off. AC denies loss of appetite or suicidal ideation.

**Additional Background Information:**  
AC reports that she had a somewhat unhappy adolescence. She was always anxious at school, worried about her grades, and also because her social relationships were not as good as she would have liked. She is currently mainly concerned about her work and has fewer worries in other areas of her life. She has good friends and a boyfriend in a two‑year relationship. A routine medical evaluation ruled out other medical causes for her symptoms, particularly the back pain. She reports consuming about two alcoholic drinks per week, usually when she goes out with her boyfriend. She does not use any other drugs.

---

**Diagnostic Conclusions**

Most Likely Diagnosis: **Adjustment Disorder**  
Reasoning: AC’s symptoms began within a few weeks of a clearly identifiable stressor (workplace demands) and include both depressive and anxious features that are not severe enough to meet criteria for a major depressive episode or generalized anxiety disorder. The duration and pattern fit an adjustment reaction.

Second Most Likely: **Generalized Anxiety Disorder**  
Reasoning: While AC’s anxiety is primarily work‑related and short‑term, the presence of persistent worry, nausea, and restlessness could be considered GAD if the anxiety were more pervasive and lasted >6 months, which is not yet established.

Third Most Likely: **Single Episode Depressive Disorder**

You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Development of ICD-11 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Mood Disorders.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the Mood Disorders or other diagnoses presented to you, no diagnosis, or a diagnosis that you feel best describes the person in the vignette but is not one of the choices available.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral: CT es una mujer casada de 40 años que trabaja como contadora para una pequeña compañía local. Se presentó con su médico de atención primaria con varios síntomas gastrointestinales como diarrea intermitente, así como dolores y malestares musculares.
Presenting Symptoms:  Al interrogarla más detalladamente, CT admite que siempre se ha caracterizado por estar preocupada, y cuando esto ocurre, experimenta varios síntomas físicos incluyendo dolores de cabeza, tensión muscular en los hombros y cuello, así como diarrea. Ella observa que sus inquietudes comienzan temprano en la mañana y también la invaden por la noche, cuando a menudo tiene dificultad para quedarse dormida.
  
 CT considera su preocupación como parte de quién es y como algo que de alguna manera podría ser útil. Ella señala que su preocupación hace que tome cuidadosas medidas para asegurar que su salud y la de su familia estén en buen estado. De la misma manera, su preocupación por hacer un buen trabajo y por las finanzas le ha garantizado que los expedientes de la empresa sean precisos. Así mismo su familia ha ahorrado fondos de manera rigurosa.
  
 Sin embargo, ella admite que su preocupación con frecuencia interfiere con sus relaciones. Por momentos se encuentra irritable, lo que desencadena peleas con su esposo e hijos. Otras veces, llama repetidamente a sus hijos para cerciorarse que están bien, al punto de llegar a fastidiarlos. Ella también expresa que preocuparse ocupa varias horas de cada día. Recientemente rechazó una oportunidad de ocupar un trabajo con más responsabilidad porque pensó que no podría desempeñarse adecuadamente y que sus preocupaciones interferirían con su capacidad para enfocarse. En ocasiones, cuando piensa cómo su preocupación interfiere con su vida, su estado de ánimo empeora. A lo largo de varios días, ella se sentía triste y de mal humor. Estos periodos “deprimentes” son muy desagradables para ella, pero relata que nunca duran más de unos días.
Additional Background Information:  CT no recuerda algún periodo en su vida en cual no se haya preocupado por alguna cosa u otra. En sus veintes, consumía alcohol en un intento sin éxito para controlar su ansiedad. Dejó de beber por completo después de casarse y tener hijos. No consume otro tipo de sustancias. La evaluación médica descartó otras condiciones médicas que podrían estar causando sus síntomas.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette at the present time?
- Single Episode Depressive Disorder
- Recurrent Depressive Disorder
- Dysthymic Disorder
- Mixed Depressive and Anxiety Disorder
- Bipolar Type I Disorder
- Bipolar Type II Disorder
- Cyclothymic Disorder
- Other Mood Disorder
- Generalized Anxiety Disorder
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- A different diagnosis not listed above
- No Diagnosis; symptoms and behaviours are within normal limits

Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Second Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

También pone un lugar para su esposa todas las noches durante la cena, pues esto lo reconforta. BC  refiere que ocasionalmente va a la iglesia local, pero debido a que él y su esposa acudieron ahí durante muchos años, evita asistir, pues salen a flote recuerdos dolorosos de su pérdida. El describe su estilo de vida como simplemente esperar a morir.  BC tiene tres hijos, ya de edad adulta que se han marchado de casa. A pesar de ver a dos de ellos varias veces por semana y ver regularmente a sus cuatro nietos, reporta no disfrutar de su compañía desde que falleció su esposa. Sus hijos están preocupados porque no ha lidiado con la pérdida de su esposa y además se encuentran frustrados porque esperaban que se sintiera mejor ahora.

Recuerda que pasaban todo el tiempo juntos y fuera de su trabajo no tenían actividades independientes uno del otro. BC is a 67‑year‑old retired man who was evaluated at a mental‑health clinic because of his ongoing acute stress and increasing social isolation since his wife died 13 months ago. He says, “I will never get over her death.”  
BC reports feeling an overwhelming emptiness in his life. He says he wakes each morning feeling drowsy. As soon as he starts thinking about his wife, he is overwhelmed by extreme sadness. He describes feeling “devastated” and has not been able to recover from this state. He mentions that he cannot stop thinking about his wife, which is causing him considerable distress. He expresses feeling as if “his heart had been taken out.”  
BC now stays at home most of the time; since his wife’s death he has lost contact with most of his social network, which he and his wife enjoyed for many years. BC says his friends gave him a lot of support at first, but they have become increasingly impatient with his feelings because they think he should have moved on and started living his life again. BC also admits that he harbors resentments toward his closest friends because they still have their wives.  
BC visits his wife’s grave almost daily, spending a couple of hours there, talking to her and sometimes crying. He also sets a place for his wife every night during dinner, which comforts him. He says he talks to her at least an hour each night, recounting all the details of his day. BC reports that he occasionally goes to the local church, but because he and his wife attended there for many years, he avoids going because it brings painful memories of his loss. He describes his lifestyle as simply waiting to die. Despite this statement, BC still cooks for himself, goes to the local store almost every day to buy the newspaper and groceries, and also takes care of his garden.  
BC has three adult children who have left home. Although he sees two of them several times a week and regularly sees his four grandchildren, he reports not enjoying their company since his wife’s death. His children are concerned because he has not dealt with the loss of his wife and are frustrated because they expected him to feel better by now.  
BC describes his relationship with his wife as extremely close. They were married for 41 years and he says, “she was everything to me.” He remembers that they spent all their time together and outside of work they had no independent activities. During a recent medical exam, his doctor found that BC is in excellent physical health but expressed concern about his emotional state. BC does not consume alcohol or drugs and does not take any medication regularly. He has no history of any psychiatric disorder.

---

**Second Most Likely: Single Episode Depressive Disorder (Major Depressive Episode)**  
Reasoning: BC exhibits a persistent depressed mood, loss of interest in social interactions, and functional impairment, which are consistent with a single depressive episode, though the grief focus is primary.

**Third Most Likely: Adjustment Disorder**  
Reasoning: If the grief symptoms were less severe or the functional impairment less pronounced, an adjustment disorder to the loss could be considered; however, the symptoms here are more Prompt:
[ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Development of ICD-11 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Mood Disorders.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the Mood Disorders or other diagnoses presented to you, no diagnosis, or a diagnosis that you feel best describes the person in the vignette but is not one of the choices available.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral:  TM es un hombre de 58 años que trabaja como maestro de preparatoria. Fue referido por su médico de atención primaria para evaluación psicológica después de un examen de rutina. El médico de atención primaria nota que TM está muy afligido por la muerte de su esposa quien murió hace 5 meses después de una larga lucha contra la leucemia.
Presenting Symptoms: TM reporta que desde la muerte de su esposa, cinco meses atrás, ha experimentado  un profundo sufrimiento por su pérdida. Menciona  sentirse extremadamente triste y que la vida no tiene sentido sin ella. Al hablar de su esposa, se altera visiblemente y llora con frecuencia a lo largo de la evaluación. TM dice que  su esposa era parte esencial de su vida y que la extraña demasiado. Describe que su actual rutina diaria consiste en pensar en ella “casi todo el tiempo”. Sus pensamientos normalmente acaban en lapsos de llanto mientras reflexiona sobre la vida sin ella. Relata que en ocasiones escucha su voz, lo que sólo lo  hace consciente de su ausencia y finalmente perpetra su soledad. Admite sentir que la vida ya no tiene ningún propósito. Esto lo atribuye a haber cuidado  de su esposa durante los últimos tres años antes de su muerte, y ahora siente un vacío.
  
 TM afirma que con frecuencia se queda en cama todo el día pensando en su esposa y se niega a contestar el teléfono porque no quiere hablar con otras personas. Establece que la gente no entiende su pérdida y se enoja cuando le dicen trivialidades como “el tiempo lo cura todo”. Aunque él y su esposa tuvieron 5 hijos quienes viven cerca y lo visitan regularmente, TM expresa que le es difícil disfrutar de su compañía especialmente porque están también muy tristes cuando lo visitan. TM admite que previo a su visita al médico local, no había salido de su casa en 3 semanas. Él depende de sus hijos para la compra de abarrotes. Declara que su estilo de vida actual contrasta contra las actividades que realizaba cuando cuidaba de su esposa, tiempo en el cual estaba sumamente ocupado atendiendo sus necesidades.
  
 TM intentó regresar al trabajo después de un mes del fallecimiento de su esposa, pero descubrió que era incapaz de planear una clase y de enfocarse en sus lecciones. Después de llorar en una clase cuyo tema hizo surgir recuerdos de su esposa, decidió tomarse el tiempo restante del periodo escolar. Menciona que no sabe si será capaz de regresar a la escuela porque no se visualiza volviendo a una vida normal como si nada hubiera pasado. El afirma, “El futuro luce negro para mí. No puedo imaginar cómo podré vivir sin ella”.
Additional Background Information: TM no tiene antecedentes psiquiátricos y gozó de una relación muy feliz y cercana con su esposa con quien estuvo casado por 30 años. Su médico de atención primaria reporta que TM goza de buena salud, aunque menciona una pérdida de peso de 2.5 kg (5.5 lbs). TM no consume alcohol u otras drogas.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette at the present time?
- Single Episode Depressive Disorder
- Recurrent Depressive Disorder
- Dysthymic Disorder
- Mixed Depressive and Anxiety Disorder
- Bipolar Type I Disorder
- Bipolar Type II Disorder
- Cyclothymic Disorder
- Other Mood Disorder
- Generalized Anxiety Disorder
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- A different diagnosis not listed above
- No Diagnosis; symptoms and behaviours are within normal limits

[FORMAT]
Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Second Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

**WARNING:** Any other output format is incorrect.
Model_Output:
**English Translation of the Vignette**

Referral: TM is a 58‑year‑old high‑school teacher. He was referred by his primary‑care physician for a psychological evaluation after a routine check‑up. The primary‑care physician noted that TM is deeply distressed by the death of his wife, who died five months ago after a long battle with leukemia.

Presenting Symptoms: TM reports that since his wife’s death, five months ago, he has experienced profound suffering over her loss. He says he feels extremely sad and that life has no meaning without her. When he talks about his wife, he visibly becomes upset and cries frequently during the assessment. TM says his wife was an essential part of his life and that he misses her too much. He describes that his current daily routine consists of thinking about her “almost all the time.” His thoughts usually end in tearful episodes while he reflects on life without her. He reports that sometimes he hears her voice, which only makes him aware of her absence and ultimately perpetuates his loneliness. He admits feeling that life no longer has any purpose. He attributes this to having cared for his wife for the last three years before her death, and now he feels a void.

TM says that he often stays in bed all day thinking about his wife and refuses to answer the phone because he does not want to talk to other people. He states that people do not understand his loss and gets angry when they say trivialities such as “time heals everything.” Although he and his wife had five children who live nearby and visit him regularly, TM says it is difficult for him to enjoy their company, especially because they are also very sad when they visit him. TM admits that prior to his visit to the local doctor, he had not left his house in three weeks. He relies on his children for grocery shopping. He declares that his current lifestyle contrasts with the activities he used to do when he was caring for his wife, a time when he was very busy attending to her needs.

TM tried to return to work one month after his wife’s death, but he found that he was unable to plan a lesson and focus on his teaching. After crying in a class whose topic brought up memories of his wife, he decided to take the rest of the school term off. He says he does not know if he will be able to return to school because he does not see himself returning to a normal life as if nothing had happened. He states, “The future looks dark to me. I can’t imagine how I will live without her.”

Additional Background Information: TM has no psychiatric history and had a very happy and close relationship with his wife, with whom he was married for 30 years. His primary‑care physician reports that TM is in good health, although he mentions a weight loss of 2.5 kg (5.5 lb). TM does not consume alcohol or other drugs.

---

**Diagnostic Conclusions**

Most Likely Diagnosis: Prolonged Grief Disorder  
Reasoning: TM meets ICD‑11 criteria for Prolonged Grief Disorder: intense yearning for the deceased, persistent preoccupation, emotional distress, functional impairment, and symptoms lasting >5 months.

Second Most Likely: Single Episode Depressive Disorder (Major Depressive Disorder)  
Reasoning: He exhibits classic depressive symptoms—persistent sadness, anhedonia, sleep disturbance, appetite change, and functional impairment—consistent with a single depressive episode.

Third Most Likely: Other Mood Disorder  
Reasoning: If the clinician prefers a broader mood‑disorder label or if the grief symptoms are not deemed specific enough, an “Other Mood Disorder” could be used to capture the overall affective **English Translation of the Vignette**

PK is a woman who lives with her husband and two young daughters, aged 7 and 10, and works in a government office. Last week she saw a gastroenterologist complaining of chronic stomach pain and was diagnosed with an ulcer. In an attempt to identify potential causes of the ulcer, the gastroenterologist asked PK about her stress levels. PK mentioned that she has felt extremely nervous and worried for the past few weeks, even though nothing unusual or bad has happened. The gastroenterologist suggested that PK speak with a mental‑health professional who could help her manage her anxiety.

During the initial mental‑health evaluation, PK admits that she worries almost all the time, and she is now distressed because her worry might harm her health. PK says she has always been “a worrier” and that she thinks this trait has helped her stay alert to her family and be aware of her work. However, she mentions that over the past two years “it has gotten out of control” and she worries to the point of feeling overwhelmed and exhausted. When asked what she worries about, she replies, “It could be anything – if my daughters get sick, if I close the shop, if I can finish the work report on time – anything I think about worries me.” She gives the example that when her daughters have a minor cold, she worries it could become serious because she has to take them to the doctor, even though she knows they are generally healthy and that children get colds. Another example: PK says her husband is very social and often invites his coworkers to dinner, which PK says “really stresses me out” because she worries the food will turn out badly and somehow could damage her husband’s career.

On most days, she finds it very difficult to fall asleep because she is in bed thinking about all the things she has to do the next day, and she worries she will not be able to meet everything. She feels very tense, and frequently has shoulder and upper back pain that she recognizes comes from tension.

PK says she and her husband have a good relationship, but recently they have argued because she is always tense and on edge. Her older daughter told her that from now on she will not say anything to PK because “she makes a big mess out of everything.” PK says she used to let some things go and had time for fun with her family, but this has become impossible over the last couple of years. She says, “They’re getting tired of my way of being.”

Additional Background Information: PK is an only child. Her parents divorced when she was very young, and she grew up with her mother and grandparents. She does not take any medications other than antacids for her ulcer. She rarely drinks alcohol – usually just one glass of wine – and has never used drugs. Her recent medical exam revealed no other abnormalities besides the ulcer.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral:   TS es una mujer de 44 años, casada, con tres hijos en edad escolar que trabaja como abogada. Ella ha buscado consulta con un psicólogo para “obtener asesoría sobre cómo mantener un balance saludable entre carrera y familia”.
Presenting Symptoms: Durante la primera evaluación, TS reporta que ha estado “bastante estresada” intermitentemente durante el último año desde que fue ascendida en el bufete de abogados en el que trabaja. TS estaba entusiasmada con el ascenso, pero encuentra que sus responsabilidades, que han aumentado substancialmente pueden ser abrumadoras. TS dice que se “preocupa bastante”  sobre si será capaz de actuar en un estándar lo suficientemente alto y en tener todo terminado. Se describe a sí misma como “una especie de fanática del control” y extremadamente detallista, pero dice que estas cualidades le ayudan a cumplir con plazos de entrega y a producir trabajo de un nivel superior. Sin embargo, últimamente ella se ha preocupado debido a que su nivel de estrés podría estar interviniendo con su capacidad de concentrarse en el trabajo y asimismo ejerciendo una carga sobre su familia. Ella menciona que, mientras que las evaluaciones de su desempeño en el trabajo han sido muy positivas,  ahora tiene menos tiempo para convivir con su esposo y ocasionalmente tiene que realizar varias actividades a la vez, como hacer su trabajo al mismo tiempo que ayuda a sus hijos con la tarea.
  
 Durante la entrevista, TS reportó que ella nunca se ha preocupado lo suficiente por estar “físicamente enferma”, aunque por momentos sí se siente demasiado tensa. Cuando se le preguntó cómo vivía la tensión, ella aclara que es “más una tensión de tipo emocional, que una tensión física”.  Ella no tiene dificultades para dormir, al afirmar que “al final del día, me siento tan cansada que no puedo pensar en otra cosa más que mi cama.” Cuando los pensamientos de todo lo que tiene que hacer “es demasiado” se repite a sí misma, “sólo respira y supéralo”. La mayoría de las veces, ella es capaz de calmarse de esta manera. En los fines de semana, ella  trata de evitar el trabajo para poder pasar tiempo con su familia, aunque esto no ha sido muy factible durante el último año.
  
 Ella menciona que pese a la carga de trabajo y sus preocupaciones, está manejando las cosas  “bastante bien en general”  y es capaz de terminar las asignaciones del trabajo y mantener bajo control las cosas en casa, aunque su esposo tiene que ayudar más que antes. TS dice que la relación con su esposo es “muy fuerte” y que él  le ofrece su apoyo, pero ella se siente mal por descuidarlo desde que empezó su nuevo trabajo. Además, TS se queja de no tener tiempo suficiente “sólo para ella”. Por ejemplo, ella solía ejercitarse con regularidad pero desde que fue ascendida en el trabajo, ha tenido mucho menos tiempo para hacerlo. Ella tiene miedo de que su nueva agenda la agote pronto, a menos que “aprenda algunas habilidades útiles” para balancear su vida y reducir sus inquietudes relacionadas con el trabajo.
Additional Background Information: TS es la mayor de cuatro hermanos. Su madre era una maestra de arte y su padre era un juez que TS describe como “brillante y altamente ambicioso”. Normalmente, ella bebe una copa de vino con su esposo en la noche, no obstante niega el uso de otras substancias. Sus evaluaciones médicas y pruebas de sangre recientes no revelaron ninguna anormalidad.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette at the present time?
- Generalized Anxiety Disorder
- Generalized Anxiety Disorder AND Panic Disorder  
- Panic Disorder

- Specific Phobia
- Specific Phobia AND Panic Disorder 
- Social Anxiety Disorder
- Social Anxiety Disorder AND Panic Disorder 
- Separation Anxiety Disorder
- Separation Anxiety Disorder AND Panic Disorder 
- Selective Mutism
- Other Anxiety and Fear-Related Disorder
- Unspecified Anxiety and Fear-Related Disorder
- A different diagnosis
- No Diagnosis

After 30 minutes in the hospital she “felt fine again,” but she was shaken by what had happened. Subsequent examinations found her to be in good health and the doctors could not identify any cardiovascular or other medical cause for her symptoms.

Six months ago, PF worked as an IT technician at a local company. He was supposed to travel to another city for a large convention. He tried to persuade his brother to accompany him, but the brother could not leave his own job. In the days before the trip, PF was extremely anxious. He described a great fear of being trapped among the convention center crowds, with no way out and no one to help. Instead of attending, he stopped going to work and was eventually fired. **Third Most Likely:**  
**Social Anxiety Disorder**  
*Reasoning:* PF expresses fear of humiliation and being judged by others, but his anxiety is more situational (crowds) than a generalized fear of social evaluation. [ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Revision of ICD-10 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Anxiety Disorders.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the anxiety disorder diagnoses presented to you, a diagnosis from another section of the classification (e.g., mood disorders), or no diagnosis.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral: VK es una niña de 10 años de edad que fue llevada a la clínica por su madre para recibir terapia. Su madre relata que VK hace berrinches muy fuertes y se altera excesivamente cuando se separan una de la otra. Este comportamiento ha ido escalando a lo largo del último año, al punto que sus padres están exhaustos y no saben cómo ayudar a su hija. La enfermera de la escuela de VK sugirió que fuera llevada a un terapeuta.
Presenting Symptoms:  La madre de VK menciona que su comportamiento ha sido problemático desde que empezó a asistir a la escuela 5 años atrás. En aquella época, VK lloraba casi diariamente en las mañanas que tenía que ir escuela pues no quería dejar a su madre. Tenía que ser cargada al autobús escolar al mismo tiempo que “lloraba y gritaba”. En los días feriados, VK  a veces se quedaba con sus abuelos durante el día, pero suplicaba constantemente ir al trabajo con su madre. Cuando su madre la obligaba a permanecer con sus abuelos, VK lloraba y gritaba hasta por una hora e insistía en llamar a su madre varias veces al día para hablar con ella.
  
 En la actualidad, VK aún se siente ansiosa cuando tiene que subir al autobús escolar y usualmente se queja de tener náuseas o de sentirse enferma en las mañanas en las que acude a la escuela. Ocasionalmente, pide visitar la clínica de la escuela porque tiene dolor de estómago o porque tiene la sensación de vomitar y dice que necesita que su madre la recoja. VK insiste en llamar a su madre al trabajo alrededor de 2 ó 3 veces al día desde la oficina de la escuela. Si su madre demora algunos minutos en recogerla del colegio, VK se torna extremadamente ansiosa, temerosa y regularmente llora. Se rehusó a ir a un viaje escolar que implicaba dormir fuera de casa por una noche. Ella no juega con sus amigos en el parque infantil si su madre no se encuentra con ella. Como consecuencia, sólo tiene pocos amigos además de sus primos. Disfruta estar con sus tías y primos maternos, pero se niega a dormir en su casa lejos de su madre.
  
 VK señala que ama a su padre y a sus abuelos pero se siente “muy unida” a su madre. Cuando se le pregunta por qué es tan difícil estar lejos de su madre, VK suspira, “porque algo le puede pasar y no regresará a casa”. Ella no tiene tales miedos cuando está cerca de su madre.
Additional Background Information: VK es hija única. Su madre refiere que alcanzó todos los hitos del desarrollo en tiempo, pero que era una niña “encimosa”. El entrenamiento para dormir sola fue especialmente difícil debido a que ella insistía en hacerlo junto con sus padres. VK no ha tenido otros problemas de salud y tampoco consume ningún medicamento. Sus padres siempre han gozado de buena salud.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette at the present time?
- Generalized Anxiety Disorder
- Generalized Anxiety Disorder AND Panic Disorder  
- Panic Disorder  
- Agoraphobia
- Agoraphobia AND Panic Disorder 
- Specific Phobia
- Specific Phobia AND Panic Disorder 
- Social Anxiety Disorder
- Social Anxiety Disorder AND Panic Disorder 
- Separation Anxiety Disorder
- Separation Anxiety Disorder AND Panic Disorder 
- Selective Mutism
- Other Anxiety and Fear-Related Disorder
- Unspecified Anxiety and Fear-Related Disorder
- A different diagnosis
- No Diagnosis

[FORMAT]
Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Second Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

**Translation of the vignette into English**

Referral: VK is a 10‑year‑old girl who was brought to the clinic by her mother for therapy. Her mother reports that VK throws very strong tantrums and becomes excessively upset when they are separated. This behavior has escalated over the past year to the point that her parents are exhausted and do not know how to help their daughter. The school nurse suggested that she be taken to a therapist.

Presenting Symptoms: VK’s mother says that her behavior has been problematic since she started school five years ago. At that time, VK cried almost daily in the mornings when she had to go to school because she did not want to leave her mother. She had to be carried onto the school bus while “crying and screaming.” On holidays, VK sometimes stayed with her grandparents during the day, but she constantly begged to go to work with her mother. When her mother forced her to stay with her grandparents, VK cried and screamed for up to an hour and insisted on calling her mother several times a day to talk to her.

Currently, VK still feels anxious when she has to get on the school bus and usually complains of nausea or feeling sick in the mornings when she goes to school. Occasionally, she asks to visit the school clinic because she has stomach pain or feels like vomiting and says she needs her mother to pick her up. VK insists on calling her mother at work about 2–3 times a day from the school office. If her mother takes a few minutes to pick her up from school, VK becomes extremely anxious, fearful, and regularly cries. She refused to go on a school trip that involved staying overnight away from home. She does not play with her friends in the playground if her mother is not with her. As a result, she only has a few friends besides her cousins. She enjoys being with her aunts and maternal cousins, but she refuses to sleep in her house away from her mother.

VK says she loves her father and her grandparents but feels “very attached” to her mother. When asked why it is so difficult to be away from her mother, VK sighs, “because something could happen to her and she won’t come back home.” She does not have such fears when she is close to her mother.

Additional Background Information: VK is an only child. Her mother reports that she reached all developmental milestones on time, but she was a “stubborn” child. Training to sleep alone was especially difficult because she insisted on doing it with her parents. VK has had no other health problems and does not take any medication. Her parents have always enjoyed good health.

---

**Diagnostic conclusions**

Most Likely Diagnosis: **Separation Anxiety Disorder**  
Reasoning: VK displays marked distress and anxiety specifically when separated from her mother, refuses to attend school or stay overnight away from home, and exhibits physical symptoms (nausea, stomach pain) that are triggered by separation. These features meet the DSM‑5 criteria for Separation Anxiety Disorder.

Second Most Likely: **Generalized Anxiety Disorder**  
Reasoning: VK’s anxiety extends beyond separation to include worries about her mother’s safety and general unease in various settings, suggesting a broader anxiety pattern that could be captured by GAD.

Third Most Likely: **Social Anxiety Disorder**  
Reasoning: VK avoids playing with peers in the playground when her mother is not present, indicating social avoidance that may reflect underlying social anxiety, though it is secondary to her You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Revision of ICD-10 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Anxiety Disorders.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the anxiety disorder diagnoses presented to you, a diagnosis from another section of the classification (e.g., mood disorders), or no diagnosis.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral:  AG y su esposa fueron referidos a un psicólogo por su médico de cabecera para asesoría marital. Ambos están a la mitad de sus 30’s y han estado casados por 9 años. No tienen hijos. La esposa de AG expresó preocupación con su médico ya que la relación con su marido se ha deteriorado porque ella siente que él la está "asfixiando" y se niega a "darle espacio."
Presenting Symptoms: AG y su esposa asisten a una sesión inicial juntos y aparentan tensión. La esposa de AG comienza a relatar sus preocupaciones relacionadas con el comportamiento de su marido, diciendo que el problema comenzó hace 3 años cuando ella cambió de puesto de trabajo a un puesto de ventas que implicó viajar varias veces al mes. Después de los primeros viajes de negocios con ningún incidente, AG comenzó a insistir que su esposa llamara a casa inmediatamente después de llegar a su destino, y de nuevo cuando llegara a su hotel. La esposa de AG se queja de que, antes de cada viaje AG suplica que no se vaya. Ella siente que esto es muy injusto, porque sabía que viajar iba a ser parte de su nuevo trabajo y que esto lo habían hablado antes de aceptar el trabajo. La esposa de AG dice que el comportamiento de su marido es tan disruptivo que ella piensa que tendrá que conseguir otro trabajo que no requiera viajar. Ella piensa que su comportamiento irracional, y ella está resentida ya que está afectando  su capacidad de tener la carrera que siempre ha querido.
  
 AG le explica al psicólogo que, a pesar de que él sabe que es irracional, está aterrorizado de que su esposa sea lastimada o asesinada cuando ella está de viaje de negocios. Mientras que ella está ausente, él reporta que casi no puede dormir durante la noche y, a menudo se despierta "con un sudor frío." En varias ocasiones, ha llamado a la habitación del hotel a media noche para asegurarse de que todavía está viva. Incluso cuando no está viajando, AG le manda mensajes a su esposa varias veces al día para asegurarse de que está bien. Si ella no responde de inmediato, llama a la oficina de su esposa y al teléfono móvil varias veces hasta que ella contesta.
  
 La esposa de AG describe cómo la relación con su marido siempre ha sido muy apasionada y al principio ella consideraba su conducta como una señal de su profundo afecto. AG interrumpe y dice: "Sabes cuánto te amo. Es sólo que no quiero perderte.” La esposa de AG responde rápidamente, diciendo: "¡Cómo puedes perderme cuando nunca me dejas fuera de tu vista!” La esposa de AG dice que a lo largo de su relación, AG siempre ha querido pasar todos sus tiempos libres juntos, y que él siempre ha sido un tanto inseguro, pero ahora se le ha ido de las manos. Al mismo tiempo, la esposa de AG indica que él siempre ha tenido confianza en la toma de decisiones sobre su propia vida, y ella no siente que  AG la controle en sus decisiones y comportamientos excepto en la medida en que implican estar lejos de él. AG menciona que él sabe que su esposa se siente frustrada por su comportamiento cuando viaja. Él no quiere hacer que ella se enoje, pero no puede dejar de hacerlo porque tiene tanto miedo de que algo pudiera  sucederle. El refiere que tiene una buena relación con su esposa, pero que está preocupado por los posibles efectos a largo plazo de la tensión relacionada con viajar.
Additional Background Information: AG es un fotógrafo profesional. Tiene un estudio fotográfico exitoso, y es querido por sus empleados. AG tuvo otras dos relaciones románticas significativas antes de conocer a su esposa. AG tiene amigos cercanos desde hace mucho tiempo. AG no toma alcohol ni consume sustancias. Presenta un ligero sobrepeso y presión arterial elevada, pero actualmente toma medicación para eso.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette at the present time?

- Unspecified Anxiety and Fear-Related Disorder

- No Diagnosis

**English Translation of the Vignette**

AG and his wife were referred to a psychologist by their primary care physician for marital counseling. Both are in their mid‑30s and have been married for nine years. They have no children. AG’s wife expressed concern to her doctor because the relationship with her husband has deteriorated; she feels that he is “suffocating” her and refuses to “give her space.”

During the initial joint session, both appeared tense. AG’s wife began to describe her concerns about her husband’s behavior, saying the problem started three years ago when she changed jobs to a sales position that required traveling several times a month. After the first business trips with no incidents, AG began insisting that she call home immediately after arriving at her destination, and again when she arrived at the hotel. She complains that before each trip AG begs her not to go. She feels this is unfair because she knew traveling would be part of her new job and they had discussed it before she accepted the position. She says his behavior is so disruptive that she thinks she will have to find another job that does not require traveling. She believes his irrational behavior is resentful and is affecting her ability to pursue the career she has always wanted.

AG explains to the psychologist that, although he knows it is irrational, he is terrified that his wife will be harmed or killed while she is traveling for business. While she is away, he reports that he can almost’t sleep at night and often wakes up “with a cold sweat.” On several occasions he has called the hotel room at midnight to make sure she is still alive. Even when she is not traveling, AG sends her messages several times a day to make sure she is okay. If she does not respond immediately, he calls her office and her cell phone repeatedly until she answers.

AG’s wife describes how their relationship has always been very passionate and at first she considered his conduct a sign of deep affection. AG interrupts and says, “You know how much I love you. It’s just that I don’t want to lose you.” AG’s wife quickly replies, “How can you lose me when you never let me out of your sight!” She says that throughout their relationship, AG has always wanted to spend all his free time together, and that he has always been somewhat insecure, but now it has gotten out of hand. At the same time, she indicates that he has always had confidence in making decisions about his own life, and she does not feel that AG controls her decisions and behaviors except when they involve being away from him. AG mentions that he knows his wife feels frustrated by his behavior when she travels. He does not want to make her angry, but he cannot stop because he is so afraid that something could happen to her. He says he has a good relationship with his wife, but he is concerned about the possible long‑term effects of the tension related to traveling.

Additional background: AG is a professional photographer with a successful studio and is well liked by his employees. He has had two other significant romantic relationships before meeting his wife. He has close friends for many years. He does not drink alcohol or use substances. He has mild overweight and elevated blood pressure, but is currently taking medication for it.

---

**Most Likely Diagnosis**  
**Generalized Anxiety Disorder**  
*Reasoning:* AG displays pervasive, excessive worry about multiple aspects of his life (wife’s safety, relationship, future career) that is difficult to control, accompanied by physical symptoms (insomnia, sweating, restlessness). The worry is not limited to a single situation and is present for an extended period, fitting GAD criteria.

**Second Most Likely**  
**Separation Anxiety Disorder**  
*Reasoning:* AG’s primary fear revolves around his wife traveling away from him, leading to intense anxiety and intrusive thoughts about harm. This pattern of anxiety specifically tied to separation from a loved one aligns with separation anxiety, though it is less prominent than the generalized worry.

**Third Most Likely**  
**Unspecified Anxiety and Fear‑Related Disorder**  
*Reasoning:* If the clinician feels that the presentation does not neatly fit any single ICD‑10 anxiety disorder (e.g., the fear is not a specific phobia, agoraphobia, or panic disorder), an [ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
 Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Revision of ICD-10 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Disorders Specifically Associated with Stress.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the stress-related diagnoses presented to you, a diagnosis from another section of the classification (e.g., mood or anxiety disorders), or no diagnosis.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral: El Sr. WM es un conductor de camiones de 45 años que fue remitido por su médico de atención primaria.  El Sr. WM mencionó que estaba teniendo problemas en su trabajo después de que tuvo un accidente en la carretera aproximadamente un año antes. Estuvo hospitalizado una semana recuperándose de las lesiones a causa del accidente, y afirmó que tenía suerte de estar vivo.
Presenting Symptoms: Desde el accidente, el Sr. WM está nervioso todo el tiempo y se sobresalta con los ruidos fuertes, sobre todo cuando camina cerca de la carretera. Cuando escucha frenar coches a altas velocidades, sus brazos instintivamente se lanzan hacia el volante y su pie pisa involuntariamente un freno imaginario. Antes de un viaje por carretera, no puede dormir debido a las constantes pesadillas sobre el accidente. Camina de un lado a otro por la casa y sufre de dolores en el pecho, concretamente en la caja torácica, ya que cuando ocurrió el accidente ésta quedó atrapada contra el volante. Ha evitado conducir durante su tiempo libre e interactuar con sus compañeros conductores porque no tolera escuchar historias de situaciones con riesgo de convertirse en accidentes de tráfico. Cuando llega el momento de subirse a su camión, da varias vueltas alrededor del vehículo antes de reunir el valor para hacerlo. Un par de veces el año pasado, no pudo controlar sus síntomas y faltó al trabajo alegando estar enfermo, lo que llevó a que perdiera encargos de trabajo y el pago por ellos.
Additional Background Information: El Sr. WM en general ha tenido una buena relación con su esposa durante veinte años a pesar de que ella empieza a desesperarse con su comportamiento, ya que sus ingresos se han visto reducidos significativamente. El Sr. WM tiene una relación distante con  sus hijos debido a sus constantes viajes. El examen neurológico y la Tomografía Axial Computarizada no revelan nada anormal. Sus costillas y demás lesiones han sanado bien y ya no deberían ocasionarle ningún dolor o molestia.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette?
- Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
- Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (CPTSD)
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- Other Disorder Specifically Associated with Stress
- Acute Stress Reaction
- A diagnosis from a different diagnostic area (e.g. mood disorders, psychotic disorders, personality disorders)
- No Diagnosis; normal reaction to stressful event(s)

Referral: Mr. WM is a 45‑year‑old truck driver who was referred by his primary‑care physician. [ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
 Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Revision of ICD-10 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Disorders Specifically Associated with Stress.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the stress-related diagnoses presented to you, a diagnosis from another section of the classification (e.g., mood or anxiety disorders), or no diagnosis.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral: El Sr. RV ha trabajado entregando cartas y paquetes de la oficina de correos los últimos 25 años. Ha sido su primer y único empleo desde que salió del colegio . En los últimos meses, sus compañeros de trabajo han notado que llega tarde, comete errores tontos, y parece que ya no disfruta su trabajo. Debido a que este comportamiento es extremadamente extraño en él, su jefe le ha insistido para que consulte a un profesional de la salud mental.
Presenting Symptoms:  A veces, el Sr. RV tiene que entregar paquetes de valor a clientes habituales y hace seis meses, durante una de estas entregas, dos hombres armados lo atacaron con un cuchillo para robarle. El reportó que no pudo sobreponerse al pánico que sintió y que experimentó la sensación de estar paralizado sin poder moverse. Ahora se pone muy nervioso y ansioso y lo invade una sensación de pánico si tiene que hacer entregas en ciertas partes de la ciudad. Pasa mucho tiempo pensando en el ataque y se cuestiona por qué tenía que pasarle a él y no a otro. Se atormenta pensando cómo podría haber evitado que sucediera. RV actualmente siente que el peligro lo acecha en diferentes situaciones, incluso haciendo cosas sencillas que antes no le preocupaban en absoluto, como cruzar una calle muy transitada. Le ha pedido a sus compañeros que se hagan cargo de sus rutas, ya que él no puede soportar pasar por el lugar donde fue atacado. También ha faltado al trabajo algunos días alegando estar enfermo, en lugar de afrontar su ansiedad.
Additional Background Information:  El Sr. RV vive con su pareja hace varios años, quien afirmó que su relación se ha vuelto muy tensa en los últimos meses debido a que RV se niega a salir de la casa. Antes, solían salir a bailar todos los viernes por la noche. No tienen hijos.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette?
- Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
- Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (CPTSD)
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- Other Disorder Specifically Associated with Stress
- Acute Stress Reaction
- A diagnosis from a different diagnostic area (e.g. mood disorders, psychotic disorders, personality disorders)
- No Diagnosis; normal reaction to stressful event(s)

[FORMAT]
Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Second Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

**WARNING:** Any other output format is incorrect.

**Translation of the vignette into English**

Mr. RV has worked delivering letters and packages for the post office for the last 25 years. It has been his first and only job since he finished school. In recent months, his coworkers have noticed that he arrives late, makes silly mistakes, and seems to no longer enjoy his work. Because this behavior is extremely unusual for him, his boss has urged him to see a mental‑health professional.  

**Presenting Symptoms:**  
Sometimes, Mr. RV has to deliver valuable packages to regular customers, and six months ago, during one of these deliveries, two armed men attacked him with a knife to rob him. He reports that he could not overcome the panic he felt and experienced a sense of being paralyzed and unable to move. Now he becomes very nervous and anxious, and a feeling of panic invades him if he has to make deliveries in certain parts of the city. He spends a lot of time thinking about the attack and questions why it had to happen to him and not to someone else. He is tormented by thoughts about how he could have prevented it. Mr. RV currently feels that danger is lurking in various situations, even doing simple things that previously did not worry him at all, such as crossing a busy street. He has asked his coworkers to take over his routes because he cannot bear to pass through the place where he was attacked. He has also missed work a few days, claiming to be sick, instead of confronting his anxiety.  

**Additional Background Information:**  
Mr. RV has lived with his partner for several years, who says that their relationship has become very tense in recent months because RV refuses to leave the house. Previously, they used to go dancing every Friday night. They have no children.  

---

**Most Likely Diagnosis:** Post‑Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)  
**Reasoning:** Mr. RV experienced a life‑threatening assault, now exhibits intrusive memories, hyperarousal, avoidance of reminders, and persistent anxiety—core criteria for PTSD.  

**Second Most Likely:** Adjustment Disorder  
**Reasoning:** The distress and functional impairment began within a few months of the traumatic event and are related to the stressor, but PTSD symptoms are more prominent.  

**Third Most Likely:** Acute Stress Reaction  
**Reasoning:** Although the event occurred six months ago, the symptoms could be considered an acute reaction that has persisted; however, the duration exceeds the typical 1‑month window, making it less likely than [ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
 Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Revision of ICD-10 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Disorders Specifically Associated with Stress.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the stress-related diagnoses presented to you, a diagnosis from another section of the classification (e.g., mood or anxiety disorders), or no diagnosis.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral: El Sr. AT es un trabajador del campo de 27 años, casado, con una hija de un año de edad. Fue remitido al equipo de salud mental de la comunidad por el líder de la aldea. Hace cinco meses, AT resultó herido cuando una mina explotó mientras estaba limpiando un terreno. Estaba cavando con una pala en un campo donde había muchos puestos militares abandonados. La explosión le lesionó un ojo y varias partes del cuerpo. Los otros trabajadores con los que estaba lo llevaron al hospital.
Presenting Symptoms: A pesar de haber sido un buen trabajador, no pudo realizar sus labores durante los tres meses siguientes a la explosión, mientras se recuperaba de sus heridas. Como no podía llevar ingresos a casa, su esposa ocupó su lugar en el campo para ayudar a la familia. Ella le animó a volver al trabajo tan pronto como se sintiera mejor, y él estuvo de acuerdo.
  
 Relata que, hace dos meses, el día que intentó reincorporarse al trabajo, empezó a sudar intensamente en cuanto tomó la pala, se sentía muy débil y sufrió de un fuerte dolor de cabeza. Además, cuando veía una pala, incluso fuera de los campos, se veía a sí mismo como antes, cavando, golpeando la pala contra algo y sufriendo la repentina explosión. Sentía que estaba tirado en el campo y sangrando por las heridas. Estas escenas se repetían en su mente, como si estuvieran sucediendo de nuevo. Le pasaba lo mismo cuando su mujer le decía que fuera a trabajar o cuando pasaba cerca del lugar donde ocurrió el accidente. Rehusaba hablar del accidente cuando su esposa lo traía a colación. Estaba tenso e irritable. Se sobresaltaba con cualquier ruido repentino o portazo. Comenzaba a evitar acercarse al lugar en donde se produjo la explosión o a usar ciertas herramientas para el trabajo del campo. Tenía pesadillas en las que se veía cavando con la pala cuando una mina explotaba, hiriéndolo gravemente. Se despertaba de pronto, temblando y sudando intensamente.
  
 Estos síntomas han persistido durante los últimos meses, hasta el momento en que fue referido a tratamiento. A pesar de estos problemas, volvió al trabajo y asumió de nuevo su papel de sostén de la familia tan pronto como sanaron sus heridas. Desde entonces, ha continuado acudiendo a su trabajo todos los días tolerando su malestar.
Additional Background Information: Ha habido muchos accidentes de minas que han resultado en lesiones y muertes en la zona. Tras su tratamiento inicial en el hospital después de la explosión, el Sr. AT regresó a su casa. La cita de seguimiento con su médico confirmó que sus heridas habían sanado. Su esposa lo apoyó en todo momento y su relación se mantuvo firme.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette?
- Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
- Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (CPTSD)
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- Other Disorder Specifically Associated with Stress
- Acute Stress Reaction
- A diagnosis from a different diagnostic area (e.g. mood disorders, psychotic disorders, personality disorders)
- No Diagnosis; normal reaction to stressful event(s)

Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]

**Translation of the vignette into English**

Referral: Mr. AT is a 27‑year‑old farm worker, married, with a one‑year‑old daughter. He was referred to the community mental health team by the village leader. Five months ago, AT was injured when a mine exploded while he was clearing a field. He was digging with a shovel in a field where there were many abandoned military posts. The explosion injured his eye and several parts of his body. The other workers with whom he was were taken to the hospital.

Presenting Symptoms: Although he had been a good worker, he could not perform his duties for the three months following the explosion while recovering from his injuries. Because he could not bring in income, his wife took his place in the field to help the family. She encouraged him to return to work as soon as he felt better, and he agreed.

He reports that two months ago, on the day he tried to return to work, he began to sweat intensely as soon as he picked up the shovel, felt very weak, and had a severe headache. In addition, when he saw a shovel, even outside the fields, he saw himself as before, digging, hitting the shovel against something, and suffering the sudden explosion. He felt as if he were lying in the field and bleeding from his wounds. These scenes repeated in his mind, as if they were happening again. The same happened when his wife told him to go to work or when he passed near the place where the accident occurred. He refused to talk about the accident when his wife brought it up. He was tense and irritable. He startled at any sudden noise or door slam. He began to avoid approaching the place where the explosion occurred or using certain tools for farm work. He had nightmares in which he saw himself digging with the shovel when a mine exploded, severely injuring him. He would wake up suddenly, trembling and sweating intensely.

These symptoms have persisted for the last few months, up to the point of referral for treatment. Despite these problems, he returned to work and resumed his role as the family's provider as soon as his wounds healed. Since then, he has continued to go to work every day, tolerating his discomfort.

Additional Background Information: There have been many mine accidents that have resulted in injuries and deaths in the area. After his initial treatment at the hospital following the explosion, Mr. AT returned home. The follow‑up appointment with his doctor confirmed that his wounds had healed. His wife supported him at all times and their relationship remained strong.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette?

- Post‑Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
- Complex Post‑Traumatic Stress Disorder (CPTSD)
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- Other Disorder Specifically Associated with Stress
- Acute Stress Reaction
- A diagnosis from a different diagnostic area (e.g. mood disorders, psychotic disorders, personality disorders)
- No Diagnosis; normal reaction to stressful event(s)

**Diagnosis**

Most Likely Diagnosis: Post‑Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)  
Reasoning: The patient exhibits classic PTSD symptoms—intrusive memories, flashbacks, nightmares, avoidance of trauma reminders, hyperarousal (startle, sweating, trembling), and symptoms lasting more than one month following a traumatic event.

Second Most Likely: Adjustment Disorder  
Reasoning: The distress is related to a significant life change (return to work after injury) and could be considered an adjustment reaction, but the presence of intrusive memories and hyperarousal points more strongly to PTSD.

Third Most Likely: Other Disorder Specifically Associated with Stress  
Reasoning: If PTSD were not considered, the symptoms could be categorized under a broader stress‑related disorder, but the specific criteria for PTSD are met, making this a less likely [ROLE]
You are a psychiatrist conducting a diagnostic assessment.

[INSTRUCTIONS]
 Thank you for participating in the Global Clinical Practice Network for the Revision of ICD-10 Mental and Behavioural Disorders. The study that we are asking you to participate in today relates to the diagnosis of Disorders Specifically Associated with Stress.

You will be presented with a case vignette. The vignettes used in this study describe people who are experiencing some symptoms and are presenting for evaluation. The vignettes are based on real people encountered in real clinical settings. Try to think about these case descriptions as if they were patients or clients from your regular clinical practice.

You will then be asked to select the most, second-most, and third-most appropriate diagnostic conclusion for the person in the vignette. You may select one of the stress-related diagnoses presented to you, a diagnosis from another section of the classification (e.g., mood or anxiety disorders), or no diagnosis.

First, translate the vignette into English, then select the most appropriate diagnoses.

[PATIENT CASE]
Referral: El Sr. DB tiene 47 años y es un trabajador independiente que opera en el mercado de valores. Fue remitido por su médico de familia debido a síntomas persistentes que comenzaron repentinamente a raíz de un accidente que tuvo lugar hace un año y que están afectando su vida de forma negativa. El Sr. DB no puede entender por qué no puede simplemente superar el incidente, y hasta ahora se resistía a ver a un profesional en salud mental.
Presenting Symptoms:  Hace un año, el Sr. DB estaba reparando un armario en la cocina de su casa cuando el techo y parte del tejado se derrumbaron, dejándolo atrapado y sintiendo que se ahogaba por la nube de polvo que se levantó con el derrumbe. Desde entonces se siente nervioso la mayor parte del día, evita estar cerca del armario de la cocina, así como en lugares públicos o muy concurridos. Se sobresalta fácilmente con ruidos inesperados. A pesar de ser un ávido cocinero, su miedo se ha extendido a la cocina. Cada vez que entra en la cocina, recuerda el incidente y siente como si estuviera sucediendo de nuevo. Ahora demanda que su esposa cocine siempre, a pesar de que antes compartían esa responsabilidad. Ella está resentida por esta nueva imposición y esto ha afectado a su relación. Él sufre de pesadillas al menos una vez a la semana en las que se ve atrapado y siente miedo y pánico. Despierta empapado en sudor de estas pesadillas. Cuando se encuentra en espacios cerrados, como un ascensor, siente una opresión en el pecho, igual que cuando ocurrió el accidente. Debido a sus problemas, ahora trabaja menos y los ingresos familiares se han visto afectados, además de que ya no es tan sociable como antes.
Additional Background Information: DB no tiene antecedentes de enfermedad mental y se enorgullece de no haber estado enfermo nunca. Es conocido en su comunidad como alguien muy fuerte que no le teme a nada. Su esposa y sus tres hijos no estaban en casa cuando el techo se vino abajo. A pesar de que gritó pidiendo ayuda, pasaron varias horas antes de que alguien avisara y lo pudieran rescatar.

Which of the following diagnostic conclusions best corresponds to the person described in the vignette?
- Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)
- Complex Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder (CPTSD)
- Prolonged Grief Disorder
- Adjustment Disorder
- Other Disorder Specifically Associated with Stress
- Acute Stress Reaction
- A diagnosis from a different diagnostic area (e.g. mood disorders, psychotic disorders, personality disorders)
- No Diagnosis; normal reaction to stressful event(s)

[FORMAT]
Most Likely Diagnosis: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [2-3 sentences]

Second Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

Third Most Likely: [diagnosis]
Reasoning: [1-2 sentences]

**WARNING:** Any other output format is incorrect.

**Translation of the vignette into English**

Referral: Mr. DB is a 47‑year‑old self‑employed stock trader. He was referred by his family physician because of persistent symptoms that began abruptly after an accident that occurred a year ago and are negatively affecting his life. Mr. DB cannot understand why he cannot simply move on from the incident, and until now he had resisted seeing a mental‑health professional.

Presenting Symptoms: A year ago, Mr. DB was repairing a wardrobe in his kitchen when the ceiling and part of the roof collapsed, trapping him and making him feel as if he was suffocating in the cloud of dust that rose with the collapse. Since then he feels nervous most of the day, avoids being near the kitchen wardrobe, as well as public or crowded places. He is easily startled by unexpected noises. Although he is an avid cook, his fear has spread to the kitchen. Every time he enters the kitchen, he recalls the incident and feels as if it is happening again. He now demands that his wife always cook, even though they previously shared that responsibility. She is resentful of this new imposition, and this has affected their relationship. He suffers from nightmares at least once a week in which he sees himself trapped and feels fear and panic. He wakes up drenched in sweat from these nightmares. When he is in closed spaces, such as an elevator, he feels chest pressure, just like when the accident happened. Because of his problems, he now works less and family income has been affected, and he is no longer as sociable as before.

Additional Background Information: DB has no history of mental illness and prides himself on never having been ill. He is known in his community as a very strong person who fears nothing. His wife and three children were not at home when the roof collapsed. Although he shouted for help, it took several hours before someone alerted and rescued him.

---

**Most Likely Diagnosis:** Post‑Traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD)  
**Reasoning:** The patient meets DSM‑5/ICD‑10 criteria for PTSD: intrusive memories, nightmares, avoidance of trauma‑related cues (kitchen, crowds), hyperarousal (startle, chest pressure), and functional impairment lasting >1 month.

**Second Most Likely:** Complex Post‑Traumatic Stress Disorder (CPTSD)  
**Reasoning:** CPTSD shares core PTSD symptoms but adds disturbances in self‑organization (affective dysregulation, negative self‑concept, relational difficulties). The relationship strain and persistent fear suggest possible CPTSD, though evidence for pervasive self‑concept issues is limited.

**Third Most Likely:** Adjustment Disorder  
**Reasoning:** Adjustment disorder involves emotional/behavioral symptoms within 3 months of a stressor, but the patient’s symptoms have persisted for a year, making this diagnosis less fitting. It is listed as a possible but less likely Desde hace seis años, es refugiada de un país de acogida, siendo originaria de un país donde existen graves conflictos civiles. Toda su familia fue arrestada y perseguida en su país de origen antes de que ella y sus hermanos lograran emigrar.
Presenting Symptoms:  Ella se describe en un estado de agotamiento total. Afirma no poder dormir bien desde que salió de su país, a lo mucho 2 ó 3 horas.
